data.title: Energy Transfer: American Molecules Are Headed East
data.description:
Introduction

I always enjoy writing articles on midstream companies like Energy Transfer, (ET), even though they are "down the road a piece" from my usual Upstream core focus. There are a couple of reasons. First for dividend/distribution seekers they are a ticket to a predictable stream of income that can either be used for current expenses or reinvested into additional shares/units. Many members of The Daily Drilling Report are seeking just this sort of regular recurring revenue and appreciate the coverage. Second, they generate a lot of interest when they go over to the free side, as ET has a huge-175k following on Seeking Alpha. What can I say, writers love to be read.

I've covered ET many times, in my time here on this platform. Most recently with a buy last October. It's up about 10% since that time, but has still managed to underperform most other big cap members of the midstream cohort on a price improvement basis. A chronic feature of ET, but hope springs eternal...and then there is that still tasty distribution yield of 7.16%. It more than beats a sock in the jaw, I'd say. Please give that article a read for a general work up on ET, as I limiting this one to Q-4 status updates and focus on an aspect of the company that has only been lightly covered previously.

One of the things that brought ET to the top of the write-up hopper were posts showing tankers coming from the Middle East to the Gulf of America-they're ubiquitous-you've seen them, to load up with oil. It got me thinking about how this might impact U.S. crude and fuels exporters. The short answer. Perhaps only modestly over the short run as U.S. infrastructure is near physical limits, as noted by S&P Global at its recent CERAweek conference in Houston.

The US crude export system is approaching its physical capacity limits as a widening US discount to international benchmarks is driving increased flows to Asia following the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, experts said March 23 at CERAWeek by S&P Global Energy. Latest data from Platts shows 38 crude fixtures booked between the USGC and Southeast Asia destinations in March so far, compared to just 13 in February.

But, ET has history of rising to meet the market with new infrastructure projects as we will see up the Q-4 update section. But first, let me review my American molecules thesis for those who haven't read my recent articles.

American molecules reign supreme

The Iran conflict has brought the vulnerability of shipping choke-points from the Middle East into sharp focus. As of today's writing, the Strait is closed as per Iran's military arm, the IRGC. U.S. has responded by blockading the country's ports, and attacking an Iranian container vessel that was attempting to run the gauntlet. As a result-and continuing the concerns shippers have had for their employees and the vessels they own, very few cargoes are making the transit of the Strait. It remains to be seen just how this impasse will be resolved, and when and "if" normal transits will resume. Talks are about to resume between the warring parties...maybe. What is increasingly apparent is shippers and exporters are seeking new supplies from the west, with a particular eye on American molecules. I think this a permanent shift, and if I am right, creates new long term opportunity for companies like ET.

The crude oil opportunity

It's almost like ET anticipated this opportunity might come with the acquisitions of Sunoco-2013, SemGroup-2019, and Houston Terminals-2023, that brought most of ET's current export capacity in-house. Now as the slide below shows the company has advantaged capacity on the Gulf and East Coasts.

It should be noted that ET Nederland and its Houston terminal has about 1/3 of the 5.5-6.0 mm BOPD export capacity currently available in the U.S. It has been taking actions to deepen harbor access, enhancing it's crude gathering network, and at its massive Nederland terminal has space for future dock expansion.

Bottom line, over the short haul I see ET having pricing power across its networks and terminals, that I don't think is factored into growth projections for the company as yet. Analyst's are bullish on the company at current prices, with median price targets of $22.00 per unit, and an outlier estimate of $25.00.

The thesis for ET

As the largest midstream operator of oil, gas, and NGL pipelines with millions of barrels of storage and export terminals, ET is in a prime position to continue its revenue and average EBITDA growth of ~4-5% over the last several years. That's not exciting, but the point can be made that we get enough excitement from our upstream portfolio, and are grateful for the relatively drama free, steady stream of quarterly distributions ET churns out. For the 4th quarter of 2025, ET generated EBITDA at a $16.8 bn annual run rate, a modest step up from the $16.0 bn for the entire year, and a solid 8% over 2024. If they can continue to come close to that performance, shares should rally toward the mid point of analyst's targets and perhaps even the outlier of $25.00 might come into view. The company noted in the call it had stepped up EBITDA guidance for 2026-

We now expect our 2026 adjusted EBITDA to range between $17.45 billion and $17.85 billion compared to the previous range of between $17.3 billion and $17.7 billion.

Quick math suggests the company is on track to meet this guidance, and conditions have only improved since it was given. About 90% of the company's revenue stream is committed to long term fee-based contracts, smoothing the fluctuations seen on the upstream side from crude and gas price ebbs.

Growth over the short term will come from the two principal areas of focus for the company. The first from delivery on its current gas focused project portfolio, now ~40% of its EBITDA. One big gas project is nearing completion with the Hugh Brinson-Permian to DFW 2.2 BCF/D line scheduled for Phase I start up in Q-4, 2026, and Phase II start up in Q-1, 2027. The other mega project, the 48" Desert Southwest Expansion of the Transwestern Line to New Mexico and Arizona is targeted for late 2029. The company also has the Mustang Draw processing plants coming on line late this year, as well as a number of direct sales to gas consumers in West Texas. Notably with firm deliveries of 900 mm scfd to the three Oracle projects, and potential with Fermi, and Cloudburst, both subject to FID.

ET is growing its NGL and Crude transport and processing capacity, with a number of projects due to come on line this year and early next year. Notable expansion on the NGL side will come from the Flexport NGL expansion at Nederland, the 70K BOPD, Sabina 2 conversion between Mont Belvieu and Nederland due middle of 2026. The Gateway NGL pipeline debottlenecking will enhance deliveries into the EPIC pipeline and is almost complete. On the crude oil side the Dakota Access Pipeline could start receiving 250K BOPD of Canadian crude. A welcome addition as Bakken crude is likely to dip this year.

A catalyst (perhaps) for ET

Last year the company suspended work on Lake Charles LNG. In the call it was mentioned discussions continue with third parties to use the existing import terminal for eventual LNG development or repurposing. I think these discussions could easily intensify with the new search to replace Middle East capacity. News of a deal to push this along for export could be a catalyst for the shares.

Risks

It's honestly hard to think of risks other than gas price fluctuations that could impact volumes temporarily. I see this as a minimal risk, but it can't be ruled out.

Some people are put off by the prodigious debt-$68 bn that ET carries. This is a consequence of the growth they've experienced over the last decade organically and through acquisitions. Their current leverage ratio of 4.0-4.5X is within company targets and their debt is rated BBB with a stable outlook.

Your takeaway

ET gets another solid buy from me this time around. As a K-1 issuing MLP it isn't right for everyone, but for those who's portfolio is positioned appropriately, it is at an attractive entry point to start a new position or make incremental adds to existing one's.

ET reports Q-1 on May 5th and if the EPS projection of $0.37 and revenue estimate of $27.58 bn are met, the shares could move higher. ET is priced competitively with other midstream companies, bringing up the bottom of the cohort.

The company's solid distribution coverage of 1.8X gives a comfort factor that the company will be able to continue pay it quarterly dividends while also covering capex and other corporate objectives.

I think the thesis for midstream companies in the U.S. has never been stronger, with ample supplies of energy, meeting increasing global demand for secure supplies. We get reminders daily how insecure some supplies are, and that doesn't appear to be changing anytime soon.

data.title: Warsh Will Change The Fed Forever
data.description:
Fed chair nominee Kevin Warsh is a fairly tight-lipped individual when it comes to public speaking. One of his gripes about the current Fed is that they do too many public engagements where they disclose their opinions on current monetary policy. That's what makes the hearing that Warsh had with the Senate Banking Committee so interesting, as it's a look into his views on monetary policy and the ongoings of the Fed. And he walked the walk in that regard at the hearing. When pressed about the ongoing case against Governor Lisa Cook, Warsh was quick with his reply:

If I stand for anything, it's the Fed should stay in its lane... it's inappropriate for me to weigh in on that.

There was impact, though. His hearing was a salvo that rattled precious metals markets, and while there was some rebound from the lows, silver fell as low as 5% on the day.

Warsh Has Laid Out His Plan: Regime Change

Warsh came out guns blazing, or at least as much as one can during a hearing to be Fed chair. He evoked his former mentor and made a pointed jab at the Senate committee.

Milton Friedman had a phrase that always stayed with me. He always worried about government officials that lured and hung around with what he called the tyranny of the status quo. Status quo practices and policies are especially harmful when the world is changing this fast.

This offers a good look into Warsh's economic outlook. He's a believer that we are going to see incredible growth out of AI and that it could bring deflationary pressures, making the Fed's job easier. When asked about the subject, he did acknowledge that the Fed has a dual mandate, and deflationary pressure may only help one side of it.

It's important to revisit the Fed's models and see whether this innovation cycle - while it could have over time improvements in the ​price level and make the Fed's job on inflation easier - there's a question about what that means for employment, which is another part of the Fed's mandate... I am more confident that there will be improved output than I am certain about when the effects ​of that would be on the labor market... the lag between the improvement on output and the effect on the labor markets, that's got to be central to the Fed's thinking.

After, many questions were asked about independence and if he is beholden to an alleged deal he brokered with President Trump. Here are two of Warsh's answers to similar questions about Fed independence.

The president never asked me to predetermine, commit, fix, decide on any interest rate decision, in any of our discussions, nor would I ever ​agree...

Presidents want lower rates but Fed independence is up to the Fed... Fed leadership has to make a decision about what’s the right thing to do.

A question was asked about Warsh's view of the Fed's rate path and whether he agreed with President Trump's previous call for a 1% Fed Funds Rate, although he declined to answer, saying:

Unlike many of my colleagues past and present, I don’t believe in forward guidance... I don’t believe that I should be previewing for you what a future decision might be.

Warsh did spend some time discussing inflation, which was interesting to note. He believes that the Fed is viewing inflation incorrectly, although it's unclear what changes he would make.

While it’s true that inflation is less problematic, meaning the rate of change in prices is less severe than it was some years ago, hard working Americans are no doubt feeling it... I think that means a regime change in the conduct of policy. I think that means a different, new inflation framework.

As a side note for investors to watch regarding inflation, this nomination of Kevin Warsh and his ideas about how the Fed measures inflation is coming at the same time as the nomination of Dr. Brett Matsumoto as Commissioner of the BLS. He is also seeking to redefine how inflation is measured, though it's unclear what those plans are since his nomination has not been confirmed yet. Inflation metrics are being attacked from both sides. It's unclear what will come of that, but I can't imagine that we will be measuring inflation the same way in a few years time.

From my view, this could be a good thing, so long as the updates are honest attempts at modernizing the data collection and formulas.

Warsh has said that a reduction in the Fed's balance sheet could cover the inflationary pressures of lowering rates artificially and reimplementing something close to ZIRP. That may sound extreme, and when it's discussed, it's discussed as a long process, but the Fed does have a lot of balance sheet that they could run off. The argument that now is a good time is supported by the $6.7T (and currently growing by $20B/week) sitting on the Fed's books.

Conclusion

Warsh aims to bring regime change to the Fed, and it's looking increasingly like he will get his nomination. I expect Warsh to leave a mark on the Fed if he is confirmed and that he will redefine how they view inflation. Between Warsh's view that the Fed is viewing and reacting to inflation incorrectly and the potential changes at the BLS via Dr. Matsumoto, who is also up for confirmation soon, how the public and the Fed view inflation may radically change in the upcoming years.

If the formulas behind CPI and PCE are changed, and the inputs used to calculate them are changed, they will be fundamentally different measures even if the names are kept. That may not be a bad thing, but it will have significant impacts on how data is interpreted moving forward.

While the hearing was heated, he seemed to get all the support he needed except from a single senator, Thom Tillis. That is the one roadblock left; Tillis is holding out until the DoJ drops their probe against current Fed chair Powell. The fate of that investigation is unclear, but if it concludes soon (and I expect it to, as does Tillis due to his pressure), then Warsh seems to be in a position to become the next Fed chair within a month. Powell's term ends May 15th, although he will stay on as acting chair until Warsh is fully confirmed.

He will be a very different Fed chair from Powell, that's for sure.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: Even If Tariffs Don't Impact Earnings, Stanley Black & Decker Still Not A Buy (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
In this article I will outline my thoughts on Stanley Black & Decker (SWK), the industrial machinery industry company so many tradesmen and tradeswomen are familiar with. I last wrote about SWK in June 2023 with this article. In the article I mentioned that I thought the stock could get to the $110 price level; however, that level was not attained. SWK only got as high as $103. Now, with recent news from SWK that Trump's tariffs would not materially affect full-year 2026 guidance, I was asked to re-evaluate the bull or bear case for the stock. I will use my technical analysis approach of considering price action, momentum, volume, and relative strength.

Chart 1: SWK Weekly with 30-week EMA, Momentum, Volume, and Relative Strength

Price action for SWK looks bullish. Since its low in April 2025, SWK has made a series of higher highs and higher lows on the weekly price chart. SWK rallied off the April 2025 lows to reach $80 in September 2025. It then pulled back to a higher low at $60 in November 2025. From there, SWK rallied to a higher high of about $93 in February of this year. Now, SWK has pulled back to another higher low above $65.

In the past few weeks, SWK has rallied to $76. This rally has allowed SWK to recapture its 30-week exponential moving average (EMA). In my price action analysis, I use the 30-week EMA as a filter to determine if the stock is trending higher. The 30-week time period offers a decent compromise between the medium- and long-term perspectives that suits my trading strategy. Being a trend-following trader, I like to own stocks that are trading above their 30-week EMA. I find that stocks that can trade above their rising 30-week EMAs often are at the beginning of substantial moves to the upside. This doesn't work out all the time, but often enough. Price action for SWK is bullish.

The next technical element I consider is momentum. I like to own stocks that have long-term bullish momentum. The Percentage Price Oscillator (PPO) is the indicator I use to measure momentum.

PPO is easy to use and understand. When the black PPO line is above the red signal line, that is an indication of bullish short-term momentum. Conversely, when the black PPO line is below the red signal line, as is the case now, that is an indication of bearish short-term momentum.

Long-term momentum is what is most important to me. When the black PPO line is above zero, that shows bullish long-term momentum. When the black PPO line has a reading below zero, like it does now with a reading of -0.285, that shows that there is bearish long-term momentum.

When using a weekly chart, you have to wait until the end of the week to make your final determination. Price could close this week below its 30-week EMA. Also, if the price does close higher still for the week, the black PPO line could end the week with a reading above zero.

Volume is the third technical element I consider when I do my technical analysis of a stock to buy. I want to own stocks that the smart money is buying. The smart money, or institutional money managers, only buy stocks that they think are undervalued.

The volume pane in Chart 1 shows black and red volume bars. The black volume bars show the number of shares traded for the week when the price closed higher for the week. The red volume bars show the number of shares traded for the week when the price closed lower for the week.

I want to see more black volume bars than red volume bars, and I want to see the black volume bars peaking above the red volume bars. There has been bullish volume activity around the three low levels I mentioned above. These were the higher lows I talked about earlier. Like many things in technical analysis and fundamental analysis, some perspective or subjectivity needs to be applied. Last week, there was a peaking red volume bar, which on the surface is bearish. However, price closed the week well off its lows, showing that money managers were accumulating shares, not dumping them on the pullback. Consequently, the recent volume picture leans bullish in my opinion.

Relative strength is the last technical element I consider when analyzing a stock to buy. I want to own stocks that are outperforming the market. The relative strength ratio in the bottom pane of Chart 1 allows me to analyze the relative strength of a stock against the SP 500 index.

When the black relative strength line is rising, that means SWK is outperforming the SP 500 index. If the line is falling, then SWK is underperforming the SP 500 index. At best, SWK is performing on par with the SP 500 index since SWK made its low in April 2025.

Since its most recent high, SWK has underperformed the SP 500 index. At this time, I can't get enthusiastic about the relative strength of SWK.

Figure 1: Seeking Alpha Factor Grades for SWK

With the recent news that the tariffs would not materially affect 2026 guidance, that caused the stock to catch a bid. From a valuation standpoint, SWK seems attractive. It has an A-grade in valuation. See Figure 1 above. However, that is the best thing that it has going for it at this time. Growth, profitability, and revisions all have middling grades. And momentum is a C, which is better than I would expect since it has both short-term and long-term bearish momentum.

If you were to focus only on the valuation component, you would be buying shares today in a company that is making a series of higher highs and higher lows. That is bullish, as I mentioned earlier. You will be buying shares of a company that is expected to earn $0.59 next quarter, which will be announced on April 29th. Full-year guidance for 2026 is $5.24, and 2027 guidance is for $6.11. The forward PE right now is just over 14. Assuming tariffs don't negatively impact earnings, any other bullish developments, such as lowering of interest rates, a quick end to the Iran situation, or bigger-than-expected GDP numbers, could cause SWK to exceed current FY guidance. From a valuation perspective, I can understand why a value investor might decide to buy some shares at this price.

As for me, I am not inclined to buy shares at this price. I am not a value-based investor. I am a trend-following trader. Right now, I think other stocks offer a better opportunity. As mentioned previously, relative strength is absent in SWK, so I need that to improve before I consider buying shares in the company. Furthermore, because momentum is not to my liking at the moment, I would wait until SWK closes the week with a price above its 30-week EMA and with the black PPO line above zero before I buy shares of SWK. Once those three parameters are met, I will reconsider buying shares in SWK.

data.title: Coinbase: Don't Enter Just Yet
data.description:
Introduction

My previous coverage on Coinbase Global Inc. (COIN) started in April of 2025, where I rated the stock a Strong Buy. Until today, Coinbase has underperformed the S&P 500 index (SPX), but in the meantime, especially until my latest writeup in June 2025, in which I issued a more cautious stance and a Hold rating, it was up around 150%. Since then, the stock has dropped almost 50% until today.

Regulation has become more favorable for Coinbase, but it is not fully resolved. The company recently received conditional OCC approval for a national trust charter, which would strengthen its institutional positioning if finalized, and broader U.S. crypto regulation is moving toward more clarity. Still, today’s New York lawsuit against Coinbase Financial Markets shows that meaningful legal and regulatory friction remains.

I believe in Coinbase in the long run. It has strong product innovation upside, and exposure to crypto markets is a plus in years where crypto does well, which I am convinced we will see again in the future. In 2026, however, I do see more weakness for crypto, and even though Coinbase will ultimately reduce the share that its crypto activities take in its business, for now that is still significant and will affect Coinbase stock. Since I cannot perfectly predict timing, I am assigning a Hold rating at this point in time.

Developments

Crypto is set for lower lows in H2 2026, and Coinbase will ultimately follow.

Historically, crypto performs poorly in midterm years, as does the stock market, and they are correlated. So far, crypto has followed the exact pattern of topping out in Q4 2025 (post-halving year) and dropping from there on. Price action remains within one standard deviation of the average of typical midterm year performance. Therefore, I believe it is better to listen to the signals than bluntly invest against them. The ongoing Iran war could be (one of) the catalyst(s) to enable further weakness. The Strait of Hormuz remains disrupted. Iran might not send delegates for negotiation if the blockage persists. Trump has said he does not want to extend the ceasefire, while both Washington and Tehran have accused the other side of violating it. That does not sound like de-escalation at all, yet markets are at all-time highs, despite potential negative impacts on the economy. There are currently no interest rate cuts expected until mid-2027, which could be far too late considering recent labor market weakness.

Since crypto lacks fundamentals, technicals play an important role in valuing them. Price has been in a downtrend starting in late 2025. There was one rebound into the 50-week moving average, which got rejected and sold off significantly from there, forming lower lows around $60k. Now we are in such a rebound again, and I expect the same to occur. We might overshoot the current trading zone again, indicated by the two blue trend lines, or even tap the 50-week MA again, but after that, it seems like price will drop again. Historically, Bitcoin (BTC-USD) drops around 80% in bear markets, and even assuming some normalization, let's say a drawdown of 60%; that will bring us down to at least $50k.

The reason why Coinbase will be affected by a weak crypto market stems from the following:

  • Transaction revenues will decline as there is much less euphoria and thereby trading volume in the crypto space.
  • Assets on the balance sheet will be marked down to reflect fallen crypto prices
  • This also affects other income and thereby EPS, which might turn out below analysts' estimates
  • The stock could project these results further into the future, and multiple compression may occur (that could then become a buying opportunity).

I do want to make it clear, however, that I am not a bear on COIN at all. There is significant long-term upside optionality behind this stock, and I do recognize that.

It is no secret that Coinbase has continuously been expanding their product offerings, which helps reduce crypto cycle dependence. 12 of these products already produce annualized revenues of over $100M, and four of them have been launched in 2025 and another four in 2024, indicating strong innovation momentum.

Subscription revenues continue to trend higher and now make up over 40% of net revenues as of FY2025. This highlights COIN's ongoing diversification efforts and provides them with stickier and more predictable sales. In theory, that should make the equity more valuable because future cash flows would need to be discounted less.

One of the most promising business lines is stablecoins. Average USDC held in Coinbase products is at an all-time high. Coinbase makes money from USDC primarily through reserve income shared with Circle, so rising balances directly support revenue. More broadly, stablecoins are increasingly being used for payments and settlement, with Visa reporting a $3.5B annualized stablecoin settlement run rate and the overall stablecoin market now above $320B.

Altogether, this can help create a strong flywheel for Coinbase, where they leverage their trust, causing higher AOP and product innovation, from which they can monetize better.

Valuation

In my view, analysts' estimates can be largely disregarded for Coinbase, considering earnings surprise history. The combination of low trailing multiples and personal conviction for crypto and Coinbase as a business to perform well sooner rather than later is a better strategy. A long investment time horizon can work for Coinbase, but it is likely to include massive volatility swings. I would therefore rather buy during low sentiment and low multiples while having high conviction, just like in April of 2025. Despite not being historically high, COIN's P/S ratio does not reflect pessimism like in the previous cycle's midterm year, 2022. Instead, it is somewhat in between euphoric highs and depressed lows, making it neither particularly compelling to buy nor to sell here.

COIN's weekly chart is neither particularly bullish nor very bearish. We can see price bouncing off strong support around $145, which has provided a base multiple times before. Since the low in early February, it seems like price is in an uptrend, though not a very clear one. RSI is creating higher highs and lower lows, which is bullish. Since early March, however, price has struggled in the $200-$215 region, creating significant upside wicks. This is also where the 200-week exponential moving average lies, potentially indicating a long-term zone price cannot convincingly close above for now. Throughout 2024 and 2025 (Bitcoin bull market years), trading Coinbase would have worked particularly well when trading weekly breakouts, as portrayed below. That setup offered a great risk-to-reward ratio. We could be working our way to breaking out of the most recent trend line this year, so that could be an idea to keep watching.

Conclusion

In my view, Coinbase has great next-cycle and long-term prospects. However, it is highly volatile and remains largely dependent on crypto markets, which themselves depend mostly on Bitcoin. I do see more weakness this year for Bitcoin, where one could get a better entry point into the stock, one with true asymmetrical upside. Still, selling now may be a little late.

It's important to mention that this is a temporary thesis. I do think Coinbase will fare well in the long run, but I believe it could have to endure a bit more adversity this year, as opposed to many analysts who believe the bottom is already in. Since there are valid arguments for the latter as well, however, I am rating the stock a Hold instead of a Sell, so investors that already have exposure can benefit from potential near-term upside.

data.title: Intuitive Surgical Q1 Earnings Review: Don't Overpay For Quality
data.description:
The numbers are out for Intuitive Surgical (ISRG), and they're great as usual, beating both EPS and revenue estimates while slightly improving its 2026 outlook. I can't say I'm surprised since the company has beat EPS estimates every quarter since Q4 2022. The market clearly isn't surprised by much, since the stock is basically flat in after-hours trading.

But my warning is this: be careful not to overpay for quality with ISRG stock.

Intuitive Surgical refers to itself as "a global technology leader in minimally invasive care and the pioneer of robotic-assisted surgery." That's true, and it has led to the company having a strong moat, with an impressive track record. Going forward, it's positioned for more growth, with the surgical robotics market expected to grow quickly over the next 10 years.

The valuation is also lower than its 10-year average. However, at the end of the day, it's still high, even for a company of this caliber. And that's what's going to keep me out of the stock for now. So, I give ISRG a Hold rating.

Now, let's talk about the earnings results and what makes Intuitive Surgical so strong.

Intuitive Surgical's Q1-2026 Results

Here are some of the most important headline numbers.

Q1-2026 revenue increased 23.1% to $2.77 billion, beating the consensus by $150 million. Q1-2026 non-GAAP EPS grew by 38.1% to $2.50. The consensus was $2.11.

Breaking down the revenue growth, systems revenue came in at $651 million, up 24.5% year-over-year and making up 23.5% of total revenues. Contributing to that growth were more da Vinci placements, a higher lease installed base, and higher average selling prices for the da Vinci system.

But the bulk of the revenue was from instruments and accessories, which grew by 23% to $1.69 billion. That growth was driven by procedure volumes from da Vinci and Ion. For those who don't know, the da Vinci is the surgical system used for minimally invasive surgeries, while Ion is another system that specializes in minimally invasive lung biopsies.

Worldwide procedures (which combine revenues from da Vinci and Ion) grew ~17% year-over-year. Ion is the faster-growing system, as its procedure count grew ~39% compared to Da Vinci's ~16%.

The da Vinci installed base grew to 11,395 in Q1, up 12% compared to 10,189 in the same period last year and up 2.6% sequentially compared to 11,106 systems as of Q4 2025. The Ion system grew faster here again, as its installed base grew 22% year-over-year to 1,041 systems. Quarter-over-quarter, that's a 4.6% growth rate compared to 995 systems at the end of Q4 2025.

Meanwhile, Intuitive Surgical's cash, cash equivalents, and investments position fell from $9.03 billion in Q4 to $7.98 billion, and a big reason for that was that ISRG bought back $1.1 billion worth of shares in Q1 (2.3 million shares).

Intuitive Surgical Slightly Raises Its 2026 Outlook

Management gave its 2026 outlook, and it's slightly better than the one it gave last quarter.

Here it is:

"Worldwide da Vinci procedure growth of approximately 13.5% to 15.5% in 2026." The previous guidance was 13-15% growth. Nonetheless, it's still lower than 2025's growth of 18%.

"Non-GAAP* gross profit margin to be within a range of 67.5% and 68.5% of revenue in 2026. This range includes an estimated impact from tariffs of 1.0% of revenue." Previous guidance was 67-68%.

"Non-GAAP* operating expense growth of 11% to 14% in 2026." The high side of operating expense growth was changed. The range was 11-15% before.

But the guidance improvement wasn't enough to send shares higher in the after-hours session.

The Moat That Earns It A High Valuation

With the recent results, TTM non-GAAP EPS sits at $9.62. At writing, ISRG is at $452 in after-hours, giving it a non-GAAP TTM P/E ratio of ~47x. That sounds crazy, but the moat helps justify lots of that premium.

ISRG has a large installed base, as we discussed earlier, and I don't think hospitals would be quick to switch to different systems, especially if the current one works well. Why would they take the chance, especially if the surgeons at that hospital are trained to use the da Vinci system? It would cost money, time, and safety. For a hospital to switch, a different product would have to be much better, but that doesn't seem to be the case, or else that would already be showing up in the numbers.

It's ISRG's relatively high R&D spending that keeps it ahead of the competition at the moment. Its R&D margin for 2025 was 13%, compared to 8.1% for Medtronic plc (MDT) and Stryker Corporation (SYK) at 6.5%. Intuitive Surgical makes only a few products but makes them well.

The Robotic Surgery Market Is Growing Fast

An article from Towards Healthcare calculated the global robotic surgery market at $13.79 billion last year and expects it to reach $16.07 billion this year. Not only that, but it expects the market to grow at a 16.54% CAGR from 2026 to 2035, reaching $63.73 billion. That's nothing to sneeze at.

Service Segment Growth

According to the report, the services segment is forecast to be the highest-growing segment of robotic surgery during the forecast period.

The article states:

As healthcare providers continue to invest in robotic surgery, the need for training, maintenance, software updates, and technical support services is rising. These services enhance the effectiveness and longevity of robotic systems, driving growth in the services segment.

ISRG can directly benefit from that demand.

General Surgery Segment Growth

The article also states that by application, the general surgery segment will have the highest CAGR until 2035 because of the advantages of robotic surgery. These advantages include "greater precision, minimally invasive techniques, and faster recovery times." It estimates that more than 30% of general surgeries in the United States will use robotic assistance by 2027.

The Problem Is The Valuation

As I mentioned before, the TTM P/E ratio is 47x if you adjust for the most recent earnings results (which haven't been updated in the valuation graphics below yet). That's actually lower than its 10-year average normalized P/E ratio of 63.8x.

But it's still high relative to EPS growth, which is expected to be in the low-mid teens over the next few years. That's why ISRG has a forward non-GAAP PEG of around 3x, giving it a D- grade from Seeking Alpha's quant system.

In the past, if you bought ISRG at a high valuation, you would have been rewarded. I don't deny that. Its 5-year returns are still half-decent at 67%, and the multiple was higher five years ago.

But I hope you'll forgive me for wanting a larger margin of safety here. I don't want to rest the bull case on ISRG continuing to beat expectations AND maintaining a very high valuation over the next few years. Both are needed to achieve good returns in the long term. Valuation compression can really mess with the return profile. I think there are other high-quality stocks out there that have better valuations relative to their growth potential.

One caveat: as you can tell from the chart above, the stock is actually near a support trend line. So if you're a bull looking for a technical entry point, now's a much better time than usual. But that's separate from my view that the longer-term returns are a bit less attractive at current prices.

The Bottom Line On ISRG Stock

Intuitive Surgical had a solid quarter, beating estimates again and raising guidance. That wasn't enough to lift the stock, though, probably due to its already-high valuation. This is a strong company with a big moat and lots of growth ahead. It's also sitting near a support level on the chart. But it just doesn't make the cut for me at 47x earnings. I'd get interested if the stock consolidates around this trend line for a year or so while the valuation catches up.

Therefore, ISRG stock gets a Hold from me.

data.title: Twilio: A Transition Story Caught Between Growth And Maturity
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Twilio (TWLO) has reached an inflection point as it is transitioning from being an emerging high-growth CPaaS disruptor to becoming a more mature platform with moderate growth and increasing profit margins. While the company was able to get some growth in 2025, with organic growth of 13%, the forward guidance of 8-9% growth indicates some slowing, which may also lead to compressed multiples until reacceleration appears. Therefore, valuation will be influenced by Twilio's ability to sustainably grow at its floor versus its peak levels, especially since the company continues to experience downward pressure on its gross margin due to rising carrier-driven A2P messaging costs. With this maturing of the business, the company's story turns into an execution story, making growth and margins the top metrics to watch for.

Two segments have the potential to create outsized value for Twilio: software mix expansion and AI monetization. High-margin products such as Verify, Segment, Voice AI, and Branded Calling are growing quickly; however, they currently represent a small portion of revenue and are unable to make a big difference in consolidated margins. Ultimately, Twilio's long-term success revolves around combining its data layer (Segment) with its communications API layer to establish itself as the foundation for AI-based customer communication. If AI agents are going to utilize real-time communications and contextual data to make decisions, then Twilio should have a distinct advantage in terms of providing access to these services. However, there has been very little evidence provided to support that AI adoption is positively impacting ARPA or usage volumes. Until there is evidence demonstrating that AI-based products are driving both a reacceleration in growth rates and margin expansion, the company's valuation is likely to stay flat. With Twilio's growth currently uncertain, I'm rating the stock a Hold with a 12-month price target of $160.

Growth Maturation

Twilio had a year of solid performance, with organic revenue increasing 13% above the originally expected range of 7-8%. Organic revenue from self-service customers rose 28% YoY in the fourth quarter. YoY revenue from ISVs grew 26%, and the number of large transactions (those greater than $500,000) completed during the fourth quarter grew 36% year-over-year. Also included in Q4 was a renewal agreement representing the largest single contract in Twilio's history, and reaching $5 billion in annual revenue is a notable achievement.

However, the 2025 story is behind us, with the market currently pricing in 2026 estimates. The guidance provided for Twilio's organic revenue growth rate for all of 2026 is more subdued, with expected organic revenue growth of 8-9% for the year. While the slowing growth rate (down from 13%) is not a disaster, it does reflect a fundamental change in how investors should view Twilio as a company. It is becoming apparent that the company will likely transition from being a high-growth disruptor to a more mature operator, and with that transition comes an inherent risk of a valuation re-rating.

One factor providing some balance to the decline in growth rates is multiproduct adoption by Twilio's customers, which grew 26% YoY. Software add-ons revenue also grew more than 20%. If an enterprise is using Flex, Segment, Voice AI, and messaging within their Twilio stack, then the switching costs are very real, and the resulting revenue stream becomes much stickier. However, even with these tailwinds, growth remains moderate and fairly priced in with a non-GAAP forward P/E of ~26x.

Margin Sensitivity

Twilio has had trouble increasing its non-GAAP gross margin above 50%, and that trend isn't expected to reverse in 2026. Most of the issue stems from Application-to-Person (A2P) messaging fees that wireless carriers charge for messages, which increase both the company's cost and revenue. Twilio's 2026 guidance calls for an additional 170 bps of gross margin compression due to these fees. While the company correctly states that there is no financial impact on gross profit dollars or operating income as a result of these fees, the message this sends to investors is that Twilio's largest and most profitable revenue stream (messaging) is subject to price increases beyond their control.

In order to reach the company's long-term non-GAAP operating margin targets of 21%-22%, Twilio needs to have a more favorable product mix. This means higher-margin products such as Twilio Verify (identity and authentication) and Branded Calling need to grow quickly enough to offset the negative contribution from the messaging business. Branded Calling grew about 600% YoY in Q4, and Twilio's Voice AI Revenue grew more than 60%, which are very strong growth rates. However, compared to the company's total revenue of ~$5 billion, the segment revenues remain relatively low. The market will likely need consistent proof that products Verify, Branded Calling, and AI Suite are improving the product mix before the market begins to price a margin re-rate into Twilio shares. This makes Twilio more of a "show me" story, although guidance suggests that it will be a while before these products have a material impact on margins. Given this, I would approach Twilio cautiously, especially after the stock's 75%+ one-year gain.

The AI Pivot

The most convincing component of Twilio's long-term story, and the one that is also the most speculative, is the combination of Segment's CDP with new voice AI and autonomous agent technologies. This is expected to be a single platform from which customers' real-time data drives their own personalization through automation of communication across all channels. The CEO has referred to Twilio as becoming "foundational infrastructure" for the AI era, and there are some arguments to support this claim. The only CPaaS provider that has Segment's unique data layer is Twilio. Therefore, if AI agents become the primary interfaces through which businesses engage with customers, then Twilio would be positioned as the infrastructure on which those AI agents operate.

However, most of these new capabilities are still in their early stages. Recently, Twilio introduced many AI-powered products into a private beta release and plans to reveal them more broadly at the SIGNAL 2026 conference in May. Voice AI grew more than 60% YoY, and Conversation Relay shows signs of momentum; however, these initiatives haven't yet proven that the features will lead to significant gains in ARPU. Due to this, a significant amount of execution risk remains. Twilio must compete with both hyperscalers and specialized AI companies for enterprise budgets for AI infrastructure and, simultaneously, maintain price discipline in its existing messaging business. Although the vision seems reasonable and encouraging, these AI products are still speculative, and Twilio is still expecting decreasing margins. This leads me to take a cautious approach to the company, where, in my view, risk/reward seems fairly priced at today's levels.

Earnings Preview

Twilio's upcoming earnings report presents an opportunity to assess how well the company has transitioned from high growth to a more disciplined, cash-generating operator. One thing to watch for is whether Twilio will continue to generate organic growth at the same rate guidance expected for Q1 (10-11%), or if it will fall back towards the lower end (8-9%) of its full-year forecast. Another thing to look for is the success of momentum-building channels such as self-service and ISVs to determine if the strong performance seen in 2025 represents a permanent shift or simply a short-term peak. With Twilio's current execution-driven story (as a result of upcoming AI products), there is substantial risk of multiple contractions if the company misses estimates. This is particularly true given that Twilio faces challenges defending its existing customer base from large hyperscalers and smaller, nimbler AI-native competitors alike.

In addition to assessing the company's ability to defend its position in the market, Twilio's upcoming quarterly report should provide clarity regarding margins, especially given the predicted 170 bps decline in gross margin driven by increasing carrier-based messaging fees. While Twilio's management continues to say that the increase in pass-through expenses related to these increases does not impact profit, the negative stigma associated with declining margins presents an obstacle to multiple expansion. To alleviate this, Twilio must provide evidence that the company's high-margin software products (Segment, Verify, and Voice AI) have continued to experience rapid growth. This could help offset margin drag from other segments and improve the current revenue mix. The market will likely have eyes on the new AI products, which will be presented in May. If the company is unable to present strong evidence that AI has the potential to create a meaningful re-acceleration of its core business, Twilio's valuation could falter. This is more significant after a year of strong gains, some of which have likely been driven by AI product potential.

Valuation

Twilio has a market cap of ~$22 billion at a current price per share of ~$146. This gives the company a price-to-trailing FCF ratio of ~21.2x against 2025 FCF of $945 million and a price-to-forward FCF of ~21.0x on guidance midpoint of $1.05 billion. Using non-GAAP earnings, the company trades at a forward non-GAAP P/E of ~26x. For example, ServiceNow (NOW) trades at a forward non-GAAP P/E multiple of ~23x, which is around Twilio's valuation despite stronger margins and revenue growth projections.

Applying a 23x forward non-GAAP multiple on non-GAAP FY 2026 income estimates of $1.05 billion (midpoint) brings the price per share to ~$160 after dividing by shares outstanding of ~151 million. This is my 12-month price target and represents ~10% upside, taking into account the speculative nature of AI initiatives and moderate growth expectations. After a sharp one-year run-up in the stock's valuation, I'm taking a more neutral stance on the stock's outlook, with the risk/reward appearing priced in at current levels. This is why I'm rating the stock a Hold.

Risks

Voice AI and Segment CDP adoption could accelerate faster than expected, driving the mix toward higher-margin software revenue. If this improves revenue mix and organic growth, it could cause Twilio's multiples to expand more than expected.

Carrier fee headwinds could prove more persistent than guided, harming gross margins and organic growth. This could offset AI potential and cause a downturn in valuation.

The macro environment is another risk, especially with a B2B business like Twilio. If corporate spending slows, it could cause reduced expectations and multiple contractions due to a weaker outlook.

Conclusion

Twilio now appears like a "show me" story versus its previous position as a proven long-term growth compounding story. The company has achieved profitability and meaningful scale; however, the long-term outlook of the company will largely depend on whether the investment in software and AI can reignite organic growth. Given the current valuation of ~26x forward non-GAAP P/E, this valuation appears fairly priced given relatively low organic growth rates. Due to the uncertainty surrounding new initiatives, I'm rating the stock a Hold with a 12-month price target of $160.

data.title: Robert Half: Well-Financed With A Debt-Free Balance Sheet
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Third Avenue Value Fund Q1 2026 Shareholder Letter.

Robert Half (RHI) is a professional staffing company specializing in temporary placements in the fields of finance and accounting, technology, and administrative and customer support. Robert Half also operates Protiviti, which provides consulting in a variety of fields, including risk management and internal audit. The U.S. staffing industry has experienced three consecutive years of muted activity following a period of unusually strong labor demand after the pandemic, when U.S. unemployment fell to multi-decade lows. As companies have been digesting excessive prior hiring of permanent employees, the use of temporary labor has suffered a significant cyclical downturn, leaving temporary workers at a historically low share of the overall workforce.

Beyond cyclical pressures, current investor concerns center on the potential for artificial intelligence to reduce demand for certain roles and automate aspects of the recruiting process. An alternative view holds that disciplines such as tax and audit require considerable professional judgment and risk assessment that extend beyond historical data and rules-based implementation, sustaining the need for human expertise. Further, proprietary information relating to individual temp employee performance in various roles, which has been accumulated by the likes of Robert Half over decades, may prove to be a valuable asset to employers in a world in which AI renders it difficult to discern fact from fiction. Additionally, although barriers to entry in staffing may seem low, Robert Half benefits from an extensive two-sided professional network, long-running proprietary candidate data and expertise in matching specific skill sets to defined client needs.

Furthermore, Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis data through March 2026 refute the notion that artificial intelligence has been playing a role in declining temporary staffing. There is also little evidence within those data series to suggest that professional services will be pervasively impacted. Meanwhile, the broader U.S. temporary workforce has recently begun to show signs of stability near 2.5 million workers.

Robert Half is extremely well-financed with a debt-free balance sheet and a substantial cash balance. The nature of the company's business requires very little capital intensity, and the company continues to produce a material amount of free cash flow, even in deeply depressed operating conditions. Management continues to strategically retain experienced recruiters despite near-term earnings pressure, recognizing that new recruiters require approximately two years to reach average productivity, and that the ability to retain recruiting staff in a cyclically depressed environment represents a strategic competitive advantage. There are also signs that Robert Half and a few industry peers have made increased efforts to hire additional recruiters in anticipation of an eventual cyclical upturn.

The company's shares trade at a low single-digit multiple of normalized cash flow. The company has generated positive annual free cash flow for more than three consecutive decades and has consistently returned much of that cash to shareholders through dividends and share repurchases. The company's dividend, which, at the present share price, offers a near-double-digit yield, is currently supported by ongoing cash generation.

data.description:
Listen on the go! A daily podcast of Wall Street Breakfast will be available by 8:00 a.m. on Seeking Alpha, iTunes, Spotify.

Good morning! Here's the latest in trending:

More uncertainty: Trump extends Iran ceasefire, but maintains Iranian port blockade. Iran seizes two ships in the Strait of Hormuz.

Senate hearing: Kevin Warsh likely to be confirmed as Fed chair despite concerns over financial disclosures and political influence.

Online safety: Australia orders Roblox (RBLX), Minecraft, Fortnite and Steam to explain how they protect kids on their platforms.

Aid and abet?

Florida has launched a criminal investigation into OpenAI (OPENAI) over the role its AI model ChatGPT possibly played in a deadly shooting at a state university last year. The probe followed prosecutors' initial review of the chat logs between ChatGPT and Phoenix Ikner, who opened fire at Florida State University last April, killing two people and injuring six others.

The allegations: "If ChatGPT were a person, it would be facing charges for murder," Florida Attorney General James Uthmeier said in a statement. "This criminal investigation will determine whether OpenAI bears criminal responsibility for ChatGPT's actions in the shooting." In a press conference, Uthmeier said ChatGPT offered "significant advice" to the gunman before the shooting on "what type of gun to use, which ammo went with which gun, whether or not a gun would be useful in short range." He added that ChatGPT "advised the shooter on what time of day would be appropriate for the shooting to interact with more people and where on campus would be the place to encounter a higher population."

Dig deeper: Florida law states that anyone who aids, abets, or counsels someone in the commission of a crime, and that crime is committed or attempted, may be considered a principal to the crime, Uthmeier noted. The state issued subpoenas to OpenAI (OPENAI) for information on its policies and internal training materials regarding user threats of harm to themselves and others as well as cooperation with law enforcement. An OpenAI spokesperson said ChatGPT was not responsible for the crime. "In this case, ChatGPT provided factual responses to questions with information that could be found broadly across public sources on the internet, and it did not encourage or promote illegal or harmful activity," the spokesperson said in a statement to the media.

Bigger picture: This is not the first time that ChatGPT has been implicated in a mass shooting. OpenAI is already facing a civil lawsuit over ChatGPT's possible role in a shooting that killed eight people in British Columbia, Canada earlier this year. The company banned the account of the shooter, Jesse Van Rootselaar, as she'd described scenarios involving gun violence to ChatGPT. Employees at the company considered alerting law enforcement, but ultimately decided not to do so. The family of a girl critically injured in the shooting sued OpenAI for failing to alert authorities, claiming that the firm had "specific knowledge of the shooter utilizing ChatGPT to plan a mass casualty event."
